fuzzy_prd:
hey team, we need a small utility for computing workspace navigation on multi-monitor setups — basically the same kind of thing we did for the session switching logic a while back, same general idea. the core ask is: given where the user currently is on their focused monitor, figure out which workspace they should land on when they hit 'go forward' or 'go back'. there's a wrapping/cycling mode some users want, and also some users have complained about accidentally spawning new empty workspaces they didn't mean to open, so we need toggles for that. the tricky part is around the edges — like what happens when you're at the very end of the id range, or when other monitors are 'in the way' numerically but shouldn't actually block navigation on your monitor. the tool should accept a JSON blob on stdin and spit out a single line with the result. errors should come out clean, not as stack traces or anything noisy. keep the core math totally separate from the input/output stuff — we may want to reuse it later. refer to the boundary handling we agreed on in the workspace id spec for min/max values. should be fully testable with a shell script that runs all the case files. let me know if anything is unclear.

one quick follow-up after the team questions: workspace ids are bounded pretty simply. the lowest valid workspace id is 1, and workspace ids must be positive integers. so if we're searching for a new empty workspace in the 'previous' direction, that search must never go below 1. if the active workspace id is already 1 and no lower free id exists, the active id is returned unchanged. on the top end, the highest valid workspace id is 2147483647, the largest representable signed 32-bit integer. when searching for a new empty workspace in the 'next' direction, the search stops at this ceiling. if no free id exists at or below this maximum, the active id is returned unchanged.

also, on the behavior side, the intended pattern is the GNOME-style workspace switching pattern described in the PRD's Background section: navigating along a focused monitor's sorted workspace id list, with boundary behavior (empty creation or cycling) when falling off either end. related to that, the order of ids in the monitor_ids input does not matter. the library treats monitor_ids as a sorted set — it always sorts them internally before performing any navigation logic. navigation steps to the immediately next or previous element in the sorted order.

for the option interaction, cycle takes precedence over both no_empty_before and no_empty_after. when cycle is true and the user is at the boundary of the focused monitor's list, navigation wraps within the monitor regardless of whether no_empty_before or no_empty_after is also true. and if cycling is enabled and the focused monitor has only a single workspace, both 'next' and 'previous' wrap onto that same workspace — the active id is returned unchanged, basically wraps to itself.

last piece is the test runner detail since that came up: the entry point is 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh'. it reads every *.json case file from a case directory (default: public_test_cases) and accepts --cases-dir <subdir> to specify an alternative. for each case it pipes the 'input' object to the adapter and writes the adapter's stdout to 'rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir-basename>/{filename.stem}@{case_index.zfill(3)}.txt'. outputs are namespaced by the case-directory basename so different runs never overwrite each other.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: compute_target_workspace(current_id: int, monitor_ids: list[int], occupied_ids: list[int], previous: bool = False, no_empty_before: bool = False, no_empty_after: bool = False, cycle: bool = False) -> int

trigger_keywords: how to find the next empty workspace id
which id is chosen when creating empty workspace forward
skip occupied ids going next
nearest free id above current
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When creating an empty workspace in the 'next' direction, the chosen id is the smallest integer strictly greater than the current active id that does not appear in occupied_ids (across all monitors). Occupied ids are skipped one by one until a free slot is found, up to the maximum id of 2147483647. If none is found, the active id is returned unchanged.

trigger_keywords: how to find the previous empty workspace id
which id is chosen when creating empty workspace backward
skip occupied ids going previous
nearest free id below current
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When creating an empty workspace in the 'previous' direction, the chosen id is the largest integer strictly smaller than the current active id that does not appear in occupied_ids (across all monitors). Occupied ids are skipped one by one going downward until a free slot is found, with a floor of 1. If none is found, the active id is returned unchanged.

trigger_keywords: what should error output look like
how to handle malformed json input
error format from the adapter
what is printed when input is invalid
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Any adapter-level failure — including unreadable input, malformed JSON, or an inconsistent state — must be rendered as a single neutral line of the form 'error=<category>' written to standard output. Runtime exception details or stack traces must never be leaked to the caller.

trigger_keywords: what is the output format
how should the result be printed
what does the adapter write to stdout
format of the answer line
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The execution adapter must print exactly one line to standard output in the form 'id=<integer>' followed by a newline character. No other output should be written on success. For example, if the computed target workspace id is 503, the output is exactly 'id=503\n'.

trigger_keywords: does navigation between monitors happen
can next jump to another monitor's workspace
are other monitors' workspaces in the navigation path
what about occupied ids from other monitors during in-monitor navigation
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Navigation within the focused monitor's own ordered list is completely independent of other monitors. When moving 'next' or 'previous' within the monitor's sorted workspace list (i.e., not at a boundary), ids from other monitors that happen to fall numerically between the current id and the next/previous monitor id are entirely ignored. They are only relevant when computing a new empty workspace id at a boundary.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go

rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

context_pointers.vague_reference: refer to the boundary handling we agreed on in the workspace id spec for min/max values
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The id range contract defined in the PRD's 'General rules' section: minimum valid id is 1, maximum valid id is 2147483647 (largest signed 32-bit integer). These bounds gate the empty-workspace search in both directions.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I can clarify right now — please dig into the existing codebase or test cases to infer the expected behavior, and flag any ambiguity as a comment on this ticket.

feature10_previous_lower_boundary.json.description: Move to the previous workspace when the active workspace is the first one on the focused monitor, empty-creation is allowed, yet no lower free slot exists. This occurs either when the active workspace is already at the lowest valid workspace id, or when every id below the active one is occupied all the way down to that minimum. With nowhere unoccupied to retreat to, the operation returns the active workspace id unchanged. The direction selector requests the previous workspace.

feature1_next_adjacent_on_monitor.json.description: Move to the next workspace when the active workspace is NOT the last one on the focused monitor. The relevant monitor exposes the sorted list of workspace ids that live on it; when at least one of those ids is greater than the active id, the operation selects the immediately following id from the monitor's own list. Workspace ids that belong to other monitors and happen to fall numerically between the active id and the next monitor id are irrelevant and are skipped, because navigation stays within the focused monitor's own ordered set of workspaces. The direction selector requests the next workspace (not the previous one).

feature2_next_create_empty.json.description: Move to the next workspace when the active workspace IS the last one on the focused monitor and creating empty workspaces ahead is allowed. In this situation the operation returns the smallest workspace id strictly greater than the active id that is not currently occupied on any monitor. Occupied ids immediately above the active id are skipped one by one until the first free id is found, so a gap between occupied workspaces is filled. If every id above is occupied right up to the largest representable id, the first free slot encountered (including that largest id itself, when unoccupied) is returned. The direction selector requests the next workspace.

feature3_next_no_empty.json.description: Move to the next workspace when the active workspace is the last one on the focused monitor but creating empty workspaces ahead is disabled. Because no further workspace exists on the monitor and the caller has opted out of opening a brand-new empty workspace in that direction, the operation makes no change and returns the active workspace id unchanged. This holds whether the monitor has several workspaces or only the single active one, and regardless of whether other monitors happen to occupy higher ids. The direction selector requests the next workspace.

feature4_next_cycle.json.description: Move to the next workspace when the active workspace is the last one on the focused monitor and cycling is enabled. With cycling, instead of creating a new empty workspace ahead, navigation wraps around to the first (lowest-id) workspace on the focused monitor. Cycling takes precedence over the disable-empty option. When the monitor holds only a single workspace, wrapping returns that same workspace. The direction selector requests the next workspace.

feature5_next_upper_boundary.json.description: Move to the next workspace when the active workspace is the last one on the focused monitor, empty-creation is allowed, yet no higher free slot exists. This occurs either when the active workspace is already at the largest representable workspace id, or when every id above the active one is occupied all the way up to that maximum. With nowhere unoccupied to advance to, the operation returns the active workspace id unchanged. The direction selector requests the next workspace.

feature6_previous_adjacent_on_monitor.js.description: Move to the previous workspace when the active workspace is NOT the first one on the focused monitor. The focused monitor exposes the sorted list of workspace ids that live on it; when at least one of those ids is smaller than the active id, the operation selects the immediately preceding id from the monitor's own list. Workspace ids that belong to other monitors and happen to fall numerically between the previous monitor id and the active id are irrelevant and are skipped, because navigation stays within the focused monitor's own ordered set of workspaces. The direction selector requests the previous workspace.

feature7_previous_create_empty.json.description: Move to the previous workspace when the active workspace IS the first one on the focused monitor and creating empty workspaces behind is allowed. In this situation the operation returns the largest workspace id strictly smaller than the active id that is not currently occupied on any monitor. Occupied ids immediately below the active id are skipped one by one until the first free id is found, so a gap between occupied workspaces is filled. The search never goes below the lowest valid workspace id. The direction selector requests the previous workspace.

feature8_previous_no_empty.json.description: Move to the previous workspace when the active workspace is the first one on the focused monitor but creating empty workspaces behind is disabled. Because no earlier workspace exists on the monitor and the caller has opted out of opening a brand-new empty workspace in that direction, the operation makes no change and returns the active workspace id unchanged. This holds whether the monitor has several workspaces or only the single active one, and regardless of whether other monitors happen to occupy lower ids. The direction selector requests the previous workspace.

feature9_previous_cycle.json.description: Move to the previous workspace when the active workspace is the first one on the focused monitor and cycling is enabled. With cycling, instead of creating a new empty workspace behind, navigation wraps around to the last (highest-id) workspace on the focused monitor. Cycling takes precedence over the disable-empty option. When the monitor holds only a single workspace, wrapping returns that same workspace. The direction selector requests the previous workspace.
